cataloging_subject.label: The Canterbury Tales
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Geoffrey Chaucer's unfinished Middle English pilgrimage frame narrative and tale collection, including its General Prologue, twenty-four pilgrim tales, linking passages, prologues, epilogues, and Retraction. It is distinct from any one manuscript witness, Robinson's 1957 eclectic scholarly edition, the 1993 OTA/U-M encoding, and either repository XML copy. The ten-fragment structure records internally linked textual groups; their complete order is not securely authorial.

catalog_summary.title: The Canterbury Tales
catalog_summary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer
catalog_summary.date: Begun in the late 1380s; worked on into the 1390s, unfinished at Chaucer's death in 1400
catalog_summary.region: England; associated with late-medieval London literary language; exact place of composition uncertain

notes: Capitalization follows the U-M item and XML header; this is the digital item's title form, not evidence of an authorial medieval title.

titles.value: Here bygynneth the Book of the tales of Cauntbury
notes: A manuscript heading in Hengwrt, not treated here as a uniquely authorial title for the abstract work.

scope.description: Robinson's 1957 source edition represented by CT, not authorship of the medieval work

date_statements.display: Begun probably in the late 1380s and worked on through the 1390s; unfinished at Chaucer's death in 1400
notes: Individual tales may predate the pilgrimage frame; exact dates are conjectural. The XML header's inherited 1387–1394 range is narrower than current scholarly caution.
The production range overlaps Chaucer's lifetime and the years immediately after his death; it is a witness date, not a composition date.

Calling Hengwrt the first known attestation relies on a qualified scholarly judgment that it is generally accepted as the earliest assembly attempt. Its [1395]–[1405] range overlaps Ellesmere's c. 1400–1410 range, so the catalog dates alone do not prove strict priority.

places.label: Southwark
notes: Narrative departure point at the Tabard Inn; not asserted as the place of composition.
London is a literary and dialect association, not a securely documented composition site for the whole work.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Robinson's edited Middle English and its digital encoding should not be conflated with the exact spelling of either Ellesmere or Hengwrt.

form_statements.description: Predominantly verse, with substantial prose in the Tale of Melibee and Parson's Tale and a prose Retraction. The XML serializes prose in L elements, which does not make those components verse.

scope.description: Especially the General Prologue's social portraits
A constituent mode, serious and parodic; not the genre of every tale
Constituent fabliaux; the Shipman's Tale and Merchant's Tale also have fabliau affinities
The Second Nun's Tale as a saint's life of Cecilia
The Prioress's Tale as a miracle of the Virgin

subject_statements.label: Religion, devotion, anticlerical satire, and penitence

summaries.value: A company of pilgrims traveling from Southwark toward the shrine of Thomas Becket at Canterbury agree to tell stories. Chaucer uses the frame to juxtapose social portraits, competing voices, literary genres, moral arguments, and performances ranging from courtly romance, a saint's life, and a Marian miracle to fabliau, beast fable, sermon, and prose instruction. The work survives unfinished and without a securely authorial final ordering of all ten fragments.

CT represents ten fragments and fifty-four titled DIV2 units: the General Prologue; twenty-four tales; tale-specific prologues, introductions, interruptions, links, and epilogues; and the Retraction. The encoded order follows Robinson's 1957 edition and its Ellesmere-related arrangement.

This record catalogs Chaucer's composite work as represented by CT. Robinson's pp. 17–265, the U-M/OTA encoding, two repository XML versions, and the Ellesmere and Hengwrt manuscript objects are transmission layers described in the linked lineage manifest rather than parts of the work itself.

extent.description: The work has twenty-four conventional pilgrim tales. CT contains ten DIV1 fragment groups and fifty-four titled DIV2 units, with 23,448 non-note L elements, 33 LG elements, and 249 PB milestones. Of those L elements, 17,414 occur in verse components and 6,034 serialize the prose Tale of Melibee, Parson's Tale, and Retraction. Ten unnumbered Latin Explicit rubrics are serialized as L inside four verse components; excluding those rubric elements yields the semantic verse-line total of 17,404 recorded here. These are encoding-derived metrics, not a new critical lineation.

content_parts.label: Fragment I (Group A)
content_parts.summary: General Prologue; Knight, Miller, Reeve, and Cook sequence. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

content_parts.label: Fragment II (Group B1)
content_parts.summary: Man of Law introduction, prologue, tale, and epilogue. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

content_parts.label: Fragment III (Group D)
content_parts.summary: Wife of Bath, Friar, and Summoner sequence. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

content_parts.label: Fragment IV (Group E)
content_parts.summary: Clerk and Merchant sequence. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

content_parts.label: Fragment V (Group F)
content_parts.summary: Squire and Franklin sequence. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

content_parts.label: Fragment VI (Group C)
content_parts.summary: Physician and Pardoner sequence. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

content_parts.label: Fragment VII (Group B2)
content_parts.summary: Shipman, Prioress, Chaucer's Sir Thopas and Melibee, Monk, and Nun's Priest sequence; verse and prose. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

content_parts.label: Fragment VIII (Group G)
content_parts.summary: Second Nun and Canon's Yeoman sequence. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

content_parts.label: Fragment IX (Group H)
content_parts.summary: Manciple prologue and tale. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

content_parts.label: Fragment X (Group I)
content_parts.summary: Parson prologue, prose tale, and prose Retraction. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

content_parts.label: The General Prologue
content_parts.summary: Robinson's I (A) unit titled “The General Prologue”; the XML represents it with 860 L elements. The L count is an encoding metric aligned to Robinson's lineation, not a new critical line count.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME repaired source XML for CT.
evidence.summary: Exact approved publication source for titles, author, language, inherited date, Robinson 1957 source edition, pp. 17–265 coverage, encoding history, structure, lineation, page milestones, and two repaired readings.

evidence.locator: count(//BODY/DIV1), count(//BODY/DIV1/DIV2), count(//LG), count(//L[not(ancestor::NOTE)]), inspection of unnumbered L rubric content, count(//PB), DIV2 headings, line @N values, and page milestones
evidence.summary: Reproducibly finds 10 fragment groups, 54 titled component units, 33 LG elements, 23,448 non-note L elements, and 249 PB milestones. Classifying Melibee, Parson, and Retraction as prose leaves 17,414 L elements in verse components and 6,034 in prose components. Ten of the verse-component L elements are unnumbered Latin Explicit rubrics—three in the Knight's Tale, four in the Clerk's Tale, two in the Squire's Tale, and one in the Canon Yeoman's Tale—so the semantic verse-line total is 17,404.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CT, The Canterbury tales.
evidence.summary: Official item record and table of contents confirming Chaucer attribution, Robinson's 1957 source, the ten-group sequence, all component titles, public full text, and the item's current corpus context.

evidence.citation: Geoffrey Chaucer, The Works of Geoffrey Chaucer, ed. F. N. Robinson, 2nd ed. (Boston: Houghton Mifflin, 1957).
evidence.summary: Bibliographic and contents evidence for the immediate printed source edition, its editor, date, complete-work context, and CT page span. Publicly searchable copies checked were restricted rather than unrestricted full scans.

evidence.citation: Harvard's Geoffrey Chaucer Website, “Fragments or Groups of Tales.”
evidence.summary: Explains the ten internally linked fragments, the absence of a secure final order, the work's unfinished state, and Robinson's decision to retain the Ellesmere-related arrangement.

evidence.citation: Helen Cooper, The Canterbury Tales, 3rd ed., Oxford Guides to Chaucer, chapter 1, “The Canterbury Tales” (Oxford University Press, 2023).
evidence.summary: Current scholarly synthesis that the work probably began in the late 1380s, continued into the 1390s and perhaps to 1400, while dates of individual tales and the chronology of the frame remain conjectural.

evidence.citation: Harvard's Geoffrey Chaucer Website, “Middle English Dialects.”
evidence.summary: Supports a London literary-language association while warning against treating modern edited spellings as a transparent record of an abstract work's place of composition.

evidence.citation: Harvard's Geoffrey Chaucer Website, “The General Prologue.”
evidence.summary: Supports the pilgrimage frame from Southwark toward Canterbury and the General Prologue's estates-satire and social-portrait functions.
Identifies the Miller, Reeve, Shipman, Summoner, and fragmentary Cook tales as fabliaux and notes fabliau elements in the Merchant's Tale.

evidence.citation: Harvard's Geoffrey Chaucer Website, “The Prioress's Tale.”
evidence.summary: Identifies the Prioress's Tale as a miracle of the Virgin, supporting miracle-tale rather than saint's-life cataloging for this component.

evidence.citation: Harvard's Geoffrey Chaucer Website, “The Second Nun's Tale.”
evidence.summary: Identifies the Second Nun's Tale as a saint's life of Saint Cecilia, supporting the component-level saint's-life genre assignment.

evidence.citation: Estelle Stubbs, “‘Here’s One I Prepared Earlier’: The Work of Scribe D and the Hengwrt Chaucer Revisited,” The Review of English Studies 58 (2007).
evidence.summary: Supports the qualified scholarly claim that Hengwrt is generally accepted as the earliest attempt to assemble The Canterbury Tales; it does not independently narrow the repository's [1395]–[1405] catalog range.

evidence.citation: National Library of Wales Archives and Manuscripts, Peniarth MS 392D, Hengwrt Chaucer.
evidence.summary: Repository evidence for shelfmark, London production, [1395]–[1405] date, incomplete Canterbury Tales content, foliation, historical shelfmark, and the opening manuscript heading.

open_questions.question: Which individual tales predate the pilgrimage frame, and how late did Chaucer continue revising the collection?
next_steps: Preserve the broad late-1380s-to-1400 range for the composite work.
Consult tale-specific textual scholarship before assigning dates to constituent tales.

open_questions.question: What order, if any, did Chaucer intend for the ten fragments?
next_steps: Treat Robinson's sequence as an editorial arrangement rather than an authorial fact.
Expose fragment labels and ordering uncertainty in the viewer.

open_questions.question: Should the twenty-four tales eventually receive independent work records with their own sources, dates, genres, and adaptation relationships?
next_steps: Retain the complete nested part inventory in CT.
Create independent records only when component-level research can avoid inheriting whole-collection claims uncritically.

notes: Robinson's editor credit is displayed because CT is textually derived from his edition; it does not make the 1957 edition the cataloging subject.
The XML's ten DIV1 labels say Group 1–10. Fragment roman numerals and Chaucer Society letter groups are scholarly crosswalks used for human readability.
Prose encoded in L elements is cataloged as prose; encoding shape is not textual form.
